Name: curvature_aware_precision_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing: Optimizing Mixed-Precision Inference for Dynamic Input Distributions
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the geometric properties of activation covariances can serve as a reliable basis for dynamically adjusting numerical precision in mixed-precision inference, leading to improved resource efficiency and accuracy in real-world applications without retraining.
Related Work: While existing methods like Spectral Quantization Routing focus on static precision allocation based on activation geometry, our proposal emphasizes dynamic adjustments during inference based on real-time geometric diagnostics. Unlike previous works, we aim to explicitly connect these geometric properties to a formal precision-switching policy, ensuring deterministic execution and low overhead.
Abstract: In the realm of machine learning, mixed-precision inference presents a promising avenue for optimizing resource utilization, particularly in latency-sensitive applications. However, existing frameworks often rely on static profiling or heuristic methods that fail to adapt to variations in input distributions. We propose a novel approach, Curvature-Aware Precision Routing (CAPR), that leverages real-time geometric diagnostics derived from activation covariances to dynamically allocate numerical precision during inference. By analyzing the curvature properties of neural network activations, CAPR identifies well-conditioned subspaces that can tolerate lower precision without compromising accuracy, while directing computational resources toward ill-conditioned areas that require higher precision. This method not only enhances throughput but also maintains strict latency constraints. We will theoretically ground our routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds and empirically validate its effectiveness through a series of experiments across various benchmarks, demonstrating significant improvements over static methods.
Experiments: 1. Implement CAPR in a single-GPU setup and evaluate on benchmark datasets like WILDS, measuring throughput and accuracy against static mixed-precision baselines.
2. Conduct ablation studies to assess the impact of different geometric diagnostics on precision allocation, focusing on the covariance condition number and spectral decay rate.
3. Test the robustness of CAPR under varying input distributions, quantifying performance degradation and recovery speed in dynamic environments.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. The effectiveness of the geometric diagnostics in real-time scenarios may vary depending on the complexity of the model and data.
2. There may be a trade-off between the computational overhead of calculating geometric properties and the benefits gained from dynamic precision switching.
3. The initial implementation might not generalize well to all architectures or tasks, necessitating further refinement and evaluation.
